name: Cova de s'Hostalet
starting_point: C/ Santa Lavinia, Costa d'en Blanes housing development
equipment: Approximately 15 m rope, one protector, one sling for the single natural anchor point, and two carabiners
description: A small but beautiful cave discovered in December 2010 near the Costa d'en Blanes urbanization, close to the Serra de s'Hostalet trail. It consists of two chambers connected by a narrow fracture requiring rope technique to navigate. The lower chamber is richly decorated with ceiling and floor formations including beautiful curtains of various sizes, stalagmites, stalactites, and eccentric formations. Animal bones scattered in the cave suggest they were thrown in through the narrow entrance. Considered of speleological and paleontological interest.
full_description:
On December 27, 2010, this small and beautiful cavern was discovered by MALLORCA VERDE. On January 2, 2011, in the company of R. Minguillón, we headed out in the rain to carry out its exploration, unaware of what the discovery would have in store for us.

Curiously, this small cavity is located very close to one of the paths that allows entry from the "Costa d'en Blanes" housing development into the "Serra de Na Burguesa," specifically the one that runs along the "Serra de s'Hostalet."

DESCRIPTION

Once at the entrance of 1.20 x 1.60 m, one can see through it that the floor is at a shallow depth. To descend, it is recommended to install the rope on a young pine tree near the entrance and place a protector on the very edge, as otherwise the rope may suffer. Once we have "landed" in the "Entrance Hall" with dimensions of 6 x 4 m, the first impression is not too promising, despite the fact that during our visit we found specimens of a type of fungus unknown to us growing near the outside light, which "obliged" us to take out the cameras and start shooting before we accidentally destroyed them. The development of the cave runs through the S/N fracture, requiring a rebelay by placing a sling around a stone that, due to its size, blocks part of the fracture, which we had to partially clear to be able to progress. It is recommended to place the descender on the long daisy chain and a carabiner on it to eliminate the braking system so that it helps us overcome this narrow passage. Once the descent is completed, we find ourselves in the "Bottom or Final Hall" of 15.5 x 7.5 m.

The clastic processes that have developed in this cavity are clearly visible, and one can appreciate how some of the ceiling formations hang diagonally instead of vertically as would be natural. The southernmost zone of the hall partially functions as a sinkhole, gradually eroding the floor, which in the future will allow the cave to continue developing. The northern zone of the hall is heavily concretioned both on the ceiling and floor, with beautiful curtains, as well as stalagmites, barriers of the same name, and stalactites — some of them eccentric. Among the curtains, some are very large for a cavern of these dimensions, and others smaller with a serrated shape.

Bones belonging to different species can be found scattered throughout the hall, some of large size, which strongly suggests they were thrown inside since the animal they belonged to could hardly have passed through the narrow breach, which in some cases does not exceed twenty centimeters in height.

TO CONCLUDE

In short, a very interesting small cavity well worth visiting, since despite its size it has sufficient characteristics — including the fact that the visitor must possess knowledge of certain vertical progression techniques to access its depths.

As it is a cavern of small dimensions, it obviously does not fill a full day of speleology, so we will have no choice but to visit some other cavity in the area. Very close to the path and at its end is the "Avenc de sa Botilla," 461483, 4379083, 347 m, DATUM ED50. And not far from it are other cavities such as "Cova/Avenc de na Picacento" and "Avenc del Pare de Son Boronat."

Cova de s'Hostalet can be considered of speleological and paleontological interest.
